title: Symplectic implosion
speaker: Lisa Jeffrey
abstract: Symplectic implosion was developed to solve the problem that the symplectic cross-section of a Hamiltonian K-space is usually not symplectic, when K is a compact Lie group. The symplectic implosion is a stratified symplectic space, introduced in a 2002 paper of the speaker with Guillemin and Sjamaar. I survey some examples showing how symplectic implosion has been used. I describe the universal imploded cross-section, which is the imploded cross-section of the cotangent bundle of a compact Lie group. Imploded cross-sections are normally not smooth manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Commutator quotient defining symplectic implosion
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates symplectic implosion by explaining that ordinary cross-sections fail to be symplectic with infinite stabilizers, so one quotients each face by the commutator subgroup of the stabilizer to obtain a stratified symplectic space.

proof_stages.label: Universal imploded cross-section and rank-two model
proof_stages.summary: The universal imploded cross-section is introduced as the implosion of the cotangent bundle of the compact group, and the SU(2) and SU(3) universal models are described, with SU(3) appearing as a quadratic cone with an isolated singularity.

proof_stages.label: Group-valued and application landscape
proof_stages.summary: The construction is extended in outline to quasi-Hamiltonian spaces, where the moment map is group-valued and the commutator map replaces the cotangent-bundle moment map, and related appearances of implosion in the literature are surveyed.

proof_stages.label: Intersection homology as the singular invariant
proof_stages.summary: Intersection cohomology is presented as a topological but not homotopy invariant theory for stratified spaces, defined using stratifications, perversities, and allowable chains measuring deviation from transversality.

proof_stages.label: Homology intersection spaces via links and blow-ups
proof_stages.summary: Banagl--Hunsicker homology intersection spaces are introduced for depth-one pseudomanifolds using links, Moore approximations, and a blow-up construction, providing a homotopy-flavored comparison object.

proof_stages.label: Isolated-cone formulas and comparison
proof_stages.summary: For spaces with an isolated cone singularity, formulas are stated for both homology intersection spaces and intersection cohomology, leading to the comparison that the two theories give different groups for the SU(3) imploded cross-section.

proof_stages.label: Mayer--Vietoris computation for the SU(3) implosion
proof_stages.summary: The SU(3) universal implosion is decomposed into pieces around the cone on the link Y, and because the relevant nonsingular pieces allow ordinary-cohomology deformation-retraction input, the intersection-cohomology Mayer--Vietoris sequence splits and yields the stated nontrivial invariant distinguishing the space from a point.

examples.name: Rank-one universal implosion for SU(2)
examples.note: The SU(2) case collapses the zero fiber to give the cone on S^3, equivalently C^2, making the commutator-quotient construction concrete without being used in the SU(3) computation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the SU(2) universal implosion by identifying T*K with K×𝔨, taking the torus preimage, collapsing K×0, and concluding it is the cone on S^3, i.e. C^2

passes.reason: The SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one minimal case and collapses to the smooth trivial model C^2.
The SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one collapse to Cone(S^3)≅C^2, a minimal smooth flat base case rather than an adversarial example.
The SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one base case, collapsing to the smooth cone on S^3, i.e. C^2, and is used illustratively.
It is the minimal rank-one nonabelian universal implosion, collapsing to the unusually smooth/trivial model C^2.
minimal-rank base case whose implosion is the flat cone C^2 on S^3, used only as an illustrative baseline
the SU(2) rank-one universal implosion collapses to the smooth trivial model C^2/cone on S^3 and is used illustratively
the SU(2) universal implosion is the minimal rank-one case and collapses to the very simple smooth cone C^2, not a refutation of a claim
Illustrative minimal-rank case whose universal implosion is the topologically trivial smooth cone C^2.
the SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one base case and collapses to the smooth trivial cone C^2, used as a simple illustration.
the SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one base case, a smooth cone C^2 with trivial topology, used illustratively
the SU(2) universal implosion is the minimal rank-one base case, a highly regular smooth cone C^2 on S^3 used as the simplest model
The SU(2) universal implosion is the rank-one/minimal case, collapsing to the standard cone on S^3, i.e. C^2, and is used as a base illustration rather than a disproof.

examples.name: Group-valued SU(2) and SU(n) sphere implosions
examples.note: For the quasi-Hamiltonian analogue K x K, the SU(2) imploded cross-section is described as S^4, with higher SU(n) analogues giving even-dimensional spheres, illustrating the group-valued version of implosion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the group-valued SU(2) case by describing the strata, the two center-point quotients, and how they assemble as the suspension of S^3 = S^4, while only citing the SU(n) generalization

passes.reason: The group-valued implosions are identified with standard spheres S^{2n}, a maximally regular symmetric family.
The group-valued SU(2) and SU(n) implosions are standard spheres such as S^4 and S^{2n}, hence highly symmetric members of a systematic family.
The group-valued implosions are identified with spheres S^4 and S^{2n}, highly symmetric standard spaces used as positive examples.
The quasi-Hamiltonian implosions are standard spheres S^4 and S^{2n}, hence maximally regular rather than adversarial.
the quasi-Hamiltonian implosions are ordinary spheres S^4 or S^{2n}, maximally symmetric members of a systematic family
the group-valued SU(n) implosions are spheres S^{2n}, a highly regular symmetric family used as examples
the group-valued implosions are presented as the systematic sphere family S^{2n}, whose sphere structure is maximally regular rather than adversarial
Illustrative SU(n) family whose imploded spaces are spheres, hence structurally maximally regular.
the group-valued implosions are identified with even-dimensional spheres, a maximally symmetric systematic family used illustratively.
the group-valued implosions are standard spheres S^{2n}, a highly symmetric infinite family used as positive examples
the group-valued implosions are spheres S^4 and S^{2n}, highly symmetric regular manifolds presented as positive examples
The group-valued implosions form a systematic SU(n) family of spheres S^{2n}, used illustratively, and spheres are maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Smooth manifolds and graphs as depth sanity checks
examples.note: An audience question uses smooth manifolds and graphs to test the definition of depth for stratified pseudomanifolds, and the speaker confirms the smooth-manifold case while cautioning about graph blow-ups.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the depth definition to confirm smooth manifolds have depth 0 and graphs depth 1, then reasons that a graph lacks the higher codimension needed for the blow-up construction

passes.reason: Smooth manifolds and graphs are used as zero- and one-depth base sanity checks for the stratified-depth definition.
Smooth manifolds of depth 0 and graphs of depth 1 are minimal sanity-check cases for the stratified-depth definition.
Smooth manifolds and graphs are low-depth baseline cases for the definition, with no singular depth or only minimal graph strata, used as sanity checks.
Smooth manifolds have depth zero and graphs are low-dimensional sanity-check/base cases for the stratified-depth definition.
smooth manifolds of depth 0 and graphs of depth 1 are lowest-depth sanity-check cases for stratified spaces
smooth manifolds have depth zero and graphs are low-depth base cases used to sanity-check the definition
smooth manifolds and graphs are used as minimal-depth base cases for the depth definition, not as counterexamples
Sanity-check examples at the bottom of the depth hierarchy: depth-0 manifolds and simplest depth-1 graphs.
smooth manifolds and graphs serve as minimal low-depth stratified sanity checks rather than adversarial examples.
smooth manifolds have depth zero and graphs are a minimal low-dimensional depth check, used only to sanity-check definitions
smooth manifolds of depth zero and graphs of depth one are low-depth base cases used only to sanity-check the definition
Smooth manifolds of depth zero and graphs of depth one are low-depth/minimal boundary sanity checks for the definition, not counterexamples.

examples.name: Blowing up a point analogy
examples.note: In response to a question, blowing up a point in an algebraic or symplectic variety is used as an analogy for the blow-up manifold appearing in the construction of homology intersection spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the point blow-up analogy by removing the point, identifying the spherical boundary, and quotienting it to projective space

passes.reason: The analogy uses blowing up an isolated point, with the point serving as a minimal-dimensional center.
Blowing up a point uses a zero-dimensional center, the minimal local model for blow-up constructions, and is used only as an analogy.
Blowing up a point uses a zero-dimensional minimal center as a local analogy for the blow-up construction.
Blowing up a point is used as the minimal-centre base model for the more general blow-up-space construction.
blowing up a point is a standard local analogy rather than a sporadic, limiting, or adversarial object

blowing up a point uses the zero-dimensional minimal center as a boundary case analogy, not as a classification exception or counterexample
Explanatory analogy using a point, the zero-dimensional minimal blow-up center.
